content:
# Genesis Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7v–8r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_013_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_013_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 8–9 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 7v–8r, West Semitic Research Project, © Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly. Folio 7v (GEN_013_page_001.jpg) is a three-column Masoretic manuscript in the standard Tiberian layout with full niqqud and cantillation marks. The image is inverted in the West Semitic Research Project photograph — when correctly oriented, the Hebrew text runs right to left across three columns. The opening of Genesis 13 is legible: the wayyiqtol וַיַּעַל (vayyaal, "and he went up") initiates the chapter; the place-names הַנֶּגֶב (the Negev), בֵּית-אֵל (Bethel), and הָעַי (Ai) are clearly visible. The divine name יְהוָה is legible at verse 4 (Abram calling on the name of the LORD) and at the close of the narrative. The phrase הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי (verse 7, "the Canaanite and the Perizzite") is readable. The כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן double simile of verse 10 is visible across the column text, with the key phrase כְּגַן-יְהוָה legible. Folio 7v carries the text through approximately verse 12. Folio 8r (GEN_013_page_002.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 13 from approximately verse 13 through verse 18, then continues into the opening of Genesis 14. The phrase רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים (verse 13) is clearly legible. The four-directional promise of verses 14–17 is visible, with the dust simile עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ (verse 16) legible in the central column. The chapter closes with verse 18: אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא, חֶבְרוֹן, מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — the oaks of Mamre, Hebron, and the altar to the LORD — all legible. The Codex Vaticanus pa_0008 (GEN_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) bears the chapter heading "CAP. XIII" at top center of the page in the three-column uncial script. The opening Ἀνέβη δὲ Αβραμ ἐξ Αἰγύπτου is readable, and the Lot narrative continues through the double simile. The Vaticanus pa_0009 (GEN_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the divine promise to Abram and the settlement at Hebron, with a chapter marker for CAP. XIII visible in the left margin confirming its continuation of the Genesis 13 text.

---

*Abram Returns from Egypt (vv. 1–4)*

**13:1** So Abram went up from Egypt — he and his wife and all that he had, and Lot with him — into the Negev.

**13:2** Now Abram was very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold.

**13:3** And he journeyed on from the Negev as far as Bethel, to the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Ai,

**13:4** to the place of the altar that he had made there at the first. And Abram called there on the name of the LORD.

---

*The Strife Between Herdsmen (vv. 5–7)*

**13:5** And Lot also, who was traveling with Abram, had flocks and herds and tents.

**13:6** And the land was not able to bear them dwelling together, for their possessions were great, so that they could not dwell together.

**13:7** And there was strife between the herdsmen of Abram's livestock and the herdsmen of Lot's livestock. Now the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land.

---

*Abram's Offer and Lot's Choice (vv. 8–13)*

**13:8** So Abram said to Lot, "Let there be no strife between you and me, and between your herdsmen and my herdsmen, for we are kinsmen.

**13:9** Is not the whole land before you? Separate yourself from me. If you go to the left, then I will go to the right; or if you go to the right, then I will go to the left."

**13:10** And Lot lifted up his eyes and saw all the plain of the Jordan, that it was well watered everywhere — like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt — all the way to Zoar. This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah.

**13:11** So Lot chose for himself all the plain of the Jordan, and Lot journeyed east. Thus they separated from each other.

**13:12** Abram settled in the land of Canaan, while Lot settled among the cities of the plain and moved his tent as far as Sodom.

**13:13** Now the men of Sodom were wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly.

---

*The LORD's Renewed Promise to Abram (vv. 14–17)*

**13:14** The LORD said to Abram, after Lot had separated from him, "Lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are — northward and southward and eastward and westward,

**13:15** for all the land that you see I will give to you and to your offspring forever.

**13:16** I will make your offspring as the dust of the earth, so that if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted.

**13:17** Arise, walk through the land, through its length and through its breadth, for I will give it to you."

**13:18** So Abram moved his tent and came and settled by the oaks of Mamre, which are at Hebron, and he built there an altar to the LORD.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Structure of Genesis 13

Genesis 13 is a study in contrasts. It is built on a single architectural decision — Abram and Lot must separate — and it organizes the narrative so that the reader sees two men in the same landscape, facing the same material reality, making choices that reveal the whole difference between life by sight and life by promise. The three translators — EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — converged on this structural reading with unusual unanimity, and their agreement is itself significant: the chapter is sufficiently transparent in its theological architecture that literal, formal, and dynamic readings arrive at essentially the same place. The disagreements this chapter generated were real but focused; they concern individual lexical and syntactic decisions rather than the fundamental orientation of any translator's reading.

The chapter has four clear movements:

**Movement 1 — Return and Worship (vv. 1–4):** Abram retraces his steps from Egypt back to the altar between Bethel and Ai. This is penitential geography — a return to the beginning, before the Egyptian episode, before the fear and the deception. He calls on the name of the LORD again. The altar stands. The LORD has not moved.

**Movement 2 — The Problem of Abundance (vv. 5–7):** The blessing of Genesis 12 has generated conflict. Abram and Lot together have too much for the land to sustain. Their abundance — the very sign of divine favor — creates the conditions for fracture. The aside about the Canaanite and Perizzite dwelling in the land tightens the stage: the land is already occupied; the two kinsmen cannot afford to fragment their household publicly in front of peoples who already hold what God has promised.

**Movement 3 — The Choice (vv. 8–13):** Abram, the elder, the one to whom the promise was made, offers Lot everything: the whole land, first choice, without condition. This is an act of faith disguised as magnanimity. Lot lifts his eyes, sees the Jordan plain — lush, watered, paradise-like, Egypt-like — and chooses it. He moves east. The narrator immediately appends the verdict: the city near which Lot will pitch his tent is "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly."

**Movement 4 — The Renewed Promise (vv. 14–18):** After Lot departs, the LORD speaks to Abram for the first time since the Egyptian episode. He commands Abram to look — north, south, east, west — all of it. The land Abram just gave away by letting Lot choose first, God now grants to Abram and his offspring forever. The promise deepens: the offspring will be as the dust of the earth. Abram is commanded to walk the land as a symbolic act of possession. He settles at Mamre, near Hebron, and builds an altar.

---

### Council Note 1 — On 13:1–2: The Return from Egypt and the Weight of Wealth

**The opening verb:** וַיַּעַל (vayyaal, "and he went up") from the root עָלָה (alah, "to ascend"). The deliberate directional echo of 12:10 — where וַיֵּרֶד אַבְרָם מִצְרַיְמָה ("and Abram went *down* to Egypt") used the descending verb יָרַד (yarad) — is unmissable. Abram went *down* to Egypt under famine pressure; he comes *up* from Egypt now. The narrative geography encodes a theological trajectory: Egypt is descent; return to the land of promise is ascent. The LXX Vaticanus (page 8, "CAP. XIII" heading confirmed legible) opens with Ἀνέβη δὲ Αβραμ ἐξ Αἰγύπτου — "And Abram went up from Egypt" — using ἀναβαίνω (anabainō, "to go up/ascend"), in full agreement with the MT's directional verb.

**Verse 2 — "very rich" vs. "very heavy":** The MT reads **וְאַבְרָם כָּבֵד מְאֹד** — literally, "and Abram was very *heavy*." The Hebrew root כָּבֵד (kaved) means "to be heavy" and is used idiomatically for great wealth. This is the same root that appeared in 12:10 as "the famine was *severe* (כָּבֵד)": the *heavy* famine drives Abram down to Egypt; he returns *heavy* with wealth. EMET preserves the literal "heavy" to make this wordplay visible across the two verses. The LXX renders πλούσιος σφόδρα ("very wealthy"), normalizing the concrete idiom into an abstract adjective. LOGOS and PNEUMA both follow the LXX's semantic range and render "very rich" and "very wealthy" respectively.

The council rules for **"very rich"** (LOGOS/PNEUMA). While EMET correctly identifies the כָּבֵד wordplay between 12:10 and 13:2, preserving "heavy" in 13:2 produces a phrase ("Abram was very heavy") that in English suggests physical weight rather than material wealth. The English reader has no access to the Hebrew semantic field of כָּבֵד; the wordplay cannot be preserved without a note. The council preserves the wordplay in this note and adopts "very rich" as the translation text. The ironic arc — the *heavy* famine that drove Abram south becomes the *heavy* wealth he carries back north — is noted here for the reader's attention.

The wealth catalogue — livestock, silver, gold — is ordered from the agrarian base to precious metals, establishing Abram as both pastoral patriarch and merchant prince. This combined abundance is the structural precondition for the conflict that follows.

---

### Council Note 2 — On 13:3–4: Returning to the Beginning

**לְמַסָּעָיו (lemaassaiv, "on/by his journeys/stages"):** The phrase וַיֵּלֶךְ לְמַסָּעָיו ("and he went on his journeys") uses the technical term מַסָּע (massa', "a stage of travel, a campsite") — the same word used for the wilderness stopping-points in Numbers 33. Abram retraces his earlier stages of travel, camp by camp, from the Negev back north to Bethel. This is not merely geographical movement; it is a deliberate narrative return to the moment before Egypt. LOGOS renders "he journeyed on" — capturing the movement without the "stages" construction, which would be awkward in English. The council adopts this rendering.

**בַּתְּחִלָּה (batekhillah, "in the beginning") and בָּרִאשֹׁנָה (barisho'nah, "at the first"):** Verse 3 says Abram returned to the place where his tent had been "at the beginning" (בַּתְּחִלָּה), and verse 4 says he reached the altar he had made "at the first" (בָּרִאשֹׁנָה). Both words mean "beginning / first," and the narrator's use of two near-synonymous terms in adjacent verses is deliberate piling up of "first/beginning" language. Abram is not building a new life after Egypt; he is returning to the life he had before Egypt — the tent, the altar, the name of the LORD. The SOPHIA translation renders "at the beginning" (v. 3) and "at the first" (v. 4), following LOGOS, to preserve the slight variation in vocabulary while maintaining both words' shared semantic weight.

---

### Council Note 3 — On 13:5–7: The Problem the Blessing Created

**Verse 6 — "the land was not able to bear them":** The verb נָשָׂא (nasa', "to lift, to carry, to bear") personifies the land as an incapable bearer: the land cannot *carry* the combined weight of their possessions. This is a remarkable construction — the land is treated as a physical entity with a load-bearing limit. Their abundance is the cause; scarcity is not the problem. The curse of too much, the conflict generated by blessing — this is the narrative's quiet irony. LOGOS renders "the land was not able to bear them dwelling together"; EMET renders the same. The council adopts this rendering as the most faithful to the Hebrew's personification.

**Verse 7 — "the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land":** The narrator's aside expands the notice of 12:6 ("the Canaanite was then in the land") by adding the Perizzite. The Perizzite (פְּרִזִּי) appears in no ethnic table in Genesis 10 and is not an otherwise-identified national group; they may be rural or open-country dwellers (possibly related to פְּרָזִי, "open-country person," as distinct from the urban Canaanite). Their presence alongside the Canaanite comprehensively occupies the land: both the dominant urban-coastal population and the rural interior population are present. The aside functions as a warning: the herdsmen's internal conflict is happening in public, in a land already occupied by peoples whose observation of this dispute is not without consequence for the promise. The council follows all three translators in preserving the temporal marker "then" without resolving its compositional or historical implications — see the corresponding note in GEN_012.

---

### Council Note 4 — On 13:8–9: Abram's Offer — The Grammar and Theology of Generosity

**אַחִים (akhim, "brothers/kinsmen"):** Abram appeals to Lot using the word "brothers" — though their precise relationship is uncle and nephew (Lot is Abram's brother's son, 12:5). The Hebrew אָח (akh) covers the full range from biological brothers to clan kin; its use here invokes the broadest, most intimate sense of family bond. LOGOS renders "kinsmen," which the council adopts: it is the most accurate English equivalent for the range of אַחִים in context — specific enough to signal family relationship, broad enough not to overclaim biological brotherhood. EMET's phrase "for we are men brothers" (preserving the Hebrew apposition of אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים) is noted as the more literal structure; the word אֲנָשִׁים ("men") may carry a connotation of standing or dignity — "we are men of standing, kinsmen."

**הִפָּרֶד נָא מֵעָלָי (hiippaared na me'alai, "separate yourself, please, from beside me"):** The Niphal imperative places the action in Lot's domain — Abram does not *separate* Lot, he asks Lot to *separate himself*. The polite particle נָא ("please / I pray") softens command into request. The SOPHIA translation follows LOGOS in rendering "Separate yourself from me" — the Niphal reflexive force is adequately conveyed in English by "yourself."

**The right-left formulation of verse 9:** Abram's offer is structured from Lot's perspective: "if *you* go to the left, *I* will go to the right." Abram subordinates his own direction entirely to Lot's choice. This is the grammatical enactment of the theological point: Abram holds the land by promise, not by sight. He can afford to be last to choose because his security does not rest on the visual quality of territory. LOGOS renders "If you take the left hand, then I will go to the right, or if you take the right hand, then I will go to the left." The council simplifies slightly: "If you go to the left, then I will go to the right; or if you go to the right, then I will go to the left" — preserving the conditional parallel structure while improving English idiom.

---

### Council Note 5 — On 13:10: The Double Simile — "Like the Garden of the LORD, Like the Land of Egypt"

Verse 10 is the literary and theological crux of the chapter, and all three translators engaged it with exceptional care. The Jordan plain (כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן, *kikkar hayarden* — literally "the disk/circle of the Jordan," the flat valley floor of the lower Jordan and Dead Sea region) is described in a double simile:

**כְּגַן-יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם** — "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt."

**First simile — כְּגַן-יְהוָה ("like the garden of the LORD"):** The Hebrew גַּן (gan, "garden") with the divine genitive יְהוָה points unmistakably to the Garden of Eden (גַּן-עֵדֶן, Genesis 2:8–15). This is not a garden like any human garden; it is the garden YHWH himself planted — the primal paradise, the world as God intended it. The comparison elevates the Jordan plain to the highest possible register of natural beauty and abundance.

The LXX Vaticanus (pa_0008, text confirmed legible) renders **ὡς παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ** — "like the paradise of God." The word παράδεισος (paradeisos, from Persian *pairidaeza*, an enclosed royal garden) is the LXX's standard translation of גַּן in the Eden narrative (Genesis 2–3); it is the word from which the English "paradise" derives. The Vaticanus thus renders the simile through its established Eden vocabulary, preserving and perhaps heightening the Eden resonance for Greek readers. The council follows the MT ("the garden of the LORD") rather than the LXX ("the paradise of God") on the ground that "garden" is more intimate and more directly Edenic than "paradise," which has accumulated theological and eschatological freight in later Christian usage. The council notes the LXX in the variant table and in this note.

**Second simile — כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם ("like the land of Egypt"):** The land of Egypt is the paradigmatic well-watered agricultural land of the ancient Near East — the Nile valley's irrigation system produced grain in conditions where the rest of the region depended on rain. But in the context of Genesis 12–13, "the land of Egypt" carries a darker resonance: Egypt is where Abram has just been. Egypt in this narrative is the land of the famine response, of the lie about Sarai, of Abram's expulsion. Deuteronomy 11:10–11 will later contrast Egypt's irrigation-dependent agriculture with Canaan's rain-dependent dependence on the LORD — Egypt represents abundance-without-God, productivity without covenant. To say the Jordan plain looks "like the land of Egypt" is to say it looks like the place from which Abram has just returned in shame.

**The pairing of Eden and Egypt:** EMET calls this "the literary and theological crux of Genesis 13" and provides the most extended analysis. PNEUMA articulates the structural irony most vividly: "two incompatible memories simultaneously" — the original paradise and the place of near-disaster. The council adopts both perspectives. The double simile places Lot's choice at the intersection of humanity's two archetypes of abundance: the divine ideal (Eden) and the human pragmatic ideal (Egypt). Both are, by the time of the narrator's writing, deeply ironic:

- The Eden garden was lost through a choice made *by sight* — "the woman saw that the tree was good for food and that it was a delight to the eyes" (Genesis 3:6). Lot lifts his eyes, sees, and chooses by identical logic.
- Egypt is the land of bondage, the place from which YHWH must rescue his people. Lot is choosing a land that looks like the very place Abram was just expelled from.

**The proleptic parenthesis:** "This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah." The narrator inserts the future into Lot's moment of visual assessment. Even as Lot's eyes behold the beauty of the plain, the reader already knows it will be destroyed. Lot chooses the best-looking land in the region; the narrator has already marked it for ruin. The verb שַׁחֵת (shachet, "to destroy/corrupt") echoes the flood narrative (Genesis 6:13, 17: God "destroyed" the earth). The plain that looks like Eden will be obliterated like Sodom. The irony is total and deliberately structured.

**כֻּלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה ("all of it well watered"):** The participle of שָׁקָה ("to water, to irrigate") — the land waters itself or is comprehensively irrigated. The council renders "well watered everywhere" (LOGOS), capturing the totality of irrigation implied by *kullah mashqeh*. EMET's "all well-watered" and PNEUMA's "how well-watered it was, everywhere" are in substantial agreement; the council adopts LOGOS's phrasing for English smoothness.

---

### Council Note 6 — On 13:11: Lot Moves East

**מִקֶּדֶם (miqedem, "toward/to the east"):** The directional phrase is grammatically ambiguous — it can mean either "toward the east" (Lot moving east from the highlands) or "from the east" (from the eastern perspective). The standard reading is "toward the east" since Lot is moving from the Bethel area toward the Jordan valley to the east. The council follows all three translators in "journeyed east" / "set out to the east."

**Eastward movement in Genesis as a theological trajectory:** EMET and LOGOS both provide the same structural observation: eastward movement in Genesis consistently maps exile and departure from divine blessing:
- Cain departs "east of Eden" (4:16)
- The Babel builders move "from the east" toward their pride-project (11:2)
- Lot "journeyed east" — away from Abram, toward Sodom

The eastward trajectory is Lot's progressive departure from the covenant center. Genesis 14:12 will place him "dwelling in Sodom"; 19:1 will place him "sitting in the gate of Sodom" — the seat of civic authority. The council notes this trajectory without importing it into the translation text; it belongs to the reader's awareness of how the chapter connects to what follows.

**"Thus they separated from each other":** וַיִּפָּרְדוּ אִישׁ מֵעַל אָחִיו ("and they parted, each man from beside his brother") — the noun אָחִיו ("his brother") echoes Abram's appeal in verse 8 ("for we are kinsmen/brothers"). The same word that was the ground of Abram's appeal for unity becomes the word that marks their separation. LOGOS renders "Thus they separated from each other"; PNEUMA renders "That is how the two men parted ways." The council adopts LOGOS's "Thus they separated from each other" for its structural elegance and its echo of the Hebrew's "each from beside his brother."

---

### Council Note 7 — On 13:12–13: Lot near Sodom; the Narrator's Verdict

**עַד-סְדֹם ("as far as Sodom"):** The preposition עַד indicates approach or proximity — Lot does not yet live *in* Sodom but camps at its edge. Genesis 14:12 will have him "dwelling in Sodom." Genesis 19:1 will have him "sitting in the gate of Sodom." The incremental proximity is deliberate and carefully staged by the narrative. The phrase captures the gradual quality of moral drift: not a single leap but a progressive drawing near.

**The verbal echo:** וַיֶּאֱהַל (vayyehal, "he pitched his tent / moved his tent") uses the same root (אָהַל) applied to Abram's tent-pitching at Bethel (12:8; 13:18). Abram pitches his tent at sacred sites near altars; Lot pitches his tent "as far as Sodom" — the threshold of wickedness. The verbal link is the narrator's quiet structural comment: the same act of tent-pitching marks two entirely different choices of dwelling-place.

LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "moved his tent as far as Sodom." EMET renders "he pitched his tent as far as Sodom." The council adopts LOGOS/PNEUMA's "moved his tent" for the opening of the verse (where it describes a completed journey of relocation) and preserves "pitched his tent" language for verse 18 (where Abram builds a settled encampment at Mamre). The distinction is slight but the contrast between the two tent-pitchings is best preserved by allowing each instance its own rendering in context.

**Verse 13 — the double characterization:** **וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד** — "Now the men of Sodom were wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly."

Two Hebrew terms for moral failure are placed in compound:
- **רָעִים (ra'im, "wicked/evil")** — the general term for moral badness; the same word used for the "wickedness of man" before the flood (6:5)
- **חַטָּאִים (hatta'im, "sinful/sinners")** — from the root חָטָא (khata', "to sin, to miss the mark"); a relational term, sin defined against a standard and directed at someone

The directional phrase **לַיהוָה ("against the LORD")** is the most theologically loaded element: Sodom's wickedness is not merely social evil; it is specifically directed against YHWH. The preposition ל here marks the object of offense. The LXX Vaticanus renders **ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ** — "before/against God" — using ἐναντίον, which can mean either "in the presence of" or "in opposition to," slightly softening the adversarial force of the MT's construction. The council follows the MT: "against the LORD."

**Syntactic note on מְאֹד ("exceedingly"):** All three translators render this intensifier differently. EMET places it at the end: "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly." LOGOS places it at the beginning: "exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD." PNEUMA renders "deeply wicked — flagrant sinners against the LORD." The Hebrew places מְאֹד (me'od) at the end of the whole compound, so that it modifies both terms: they were *exceedingly* wicked *and* sinful. EMET's end-placement faithfully reflects the Hebrew word order. The council adopts EMET's word order: **"wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly"** — the intensifier falls at the end, as in the Hebrew, adding its weight to the entire compound rather than to the first term alone.

---

### Council Note 8 — On 13:14: The Divine Speech — Timing and Contrast

**אַחֲרֵי הִפָּרֶד-לוֹט מֵעִמּוֹ ("after Lot had separated from him"):** The temporal particle אַחֲרֵי ("after") is the structural hinge of the divine speech. The LORD does not speak before the separation — not to direct it; not during Lot's choice — not to intervene; but *after* Lot has departed. The timing is deliberate. The land that Abram just gave Lot first choice of, the LORD now confirms entirely to Abram. The sequence is:

1. Lot looks and chooses (v. 10–11)
2. Lot departs (v. 11–12)
3. The LORD speaks (v. 14–17)

What Abram gave away by letting another choose first, he receives from the LORD after the choosing is done. This is the structural demonstration of Abram's faith: he held the land by promise, not by sight, which is why he could afford to let Lot take what appeared best.

**The four directions:** "northward and southward and eastward and westward" (צָפֹנָה וְנֶגְבָּה וְקֵדְמָה וְיָמָּה) — the complete compass, totality. The divine survey from Abram's position encompasses more than Lot's self-directed gaze in verse 10. Lot looked at one sector — the Jordan plain — motivated by its visible appeal. The LORD invites Abram to look in every direction at once, comprehensively, at the full visible expanse of the land. Lot's sight was selective and self-interested; Abram's commanded vision is total and gift-oriented.

The council note on the connection between "Lot lifted up his eyes" (v. 10) and "Lift up your eyes" (v. 14) is adopted from LOGOS and PNEUMA: the same phrase (נָשָׂא עֵינַיִם, "to lift up the eyes"), the same deliberate act of looking — but the contrast is absolute. Lot lifts his eyes on his own initiative, motivated by visible advantage. Abram lifts his eyes at divine command, to receive a gift larger than anything Lot chose.

---

### Council Note 9 — On 13:15–16: The Land and Seed Promises

**עַד-עוֹלָם ("forever"):** The temporal term עוֹלָם (olam) designates the indefinitely long future, the furthest horizon of temporal imagination. This is its first use in connection with the land promise. The promise is not temporary or conditional on any circumstance mentioned here; it is given without limit. All three translators agree on "forever."

**כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ ("like the dust of the earth") — the most significant LXX divergence in this chapter:** The MT reads כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ (ka'afar ha'arets, "like the dust of the earth"). The LXX Vaticanus renders **ὡς τὴν ἄμμον τῆς γῆς** — "like the *sand* of the earth" — substituting ἄμμος (sand) for עָפָר (dust). This is a meaningful interpretive shift, not a scribal accident. The Hebrew עָפָר (afar) is the fine granular dust of dry ground — specifically the material from which Adam was formed in Genesis 2:7 ("God formed man from the *dust* of the ground," עָפָר מִן-הָאֲדָמָה) and to which humanity returns in the curse of 3:19 ("for dust you are and to dust you shall return"). The dust simile for Abram's offspring thus carries a double resonance:
- It connects to the creation of humanity from dust (2:7): Abram's descendants will be as numerous as the very material from which every human being was made
- It inverts the curse-image (dust = mortality, 3:19) into a blessing-image (dust = innumerability, life beyond counting)

The LXX's "sand" is more straightforwardly positive — sand lacks dust's death-associations — but it loses the Adam/creation connection. EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all follow the MT ("dust of the earth") with unanimity; the council concurs. The LXX variant is noted in the table.

**The conditional impossibility of verse 16:** "if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted" — this is a rhetorical condition whose premise is impossible. No one can count dust; therefore no one will count Abram's offspring. The absolute uncountability of dust is the measure of the absolute innumerability of the promised descendants. The later Abrahamic promises will use different images for the same innumerability: stars of heaven (Genesis 15:5), and stars and sand of the seashore (Genesis 22:17). The dust of the earth (Genesis 13), the stars of heaven (Genesis 15), and the sand of the seashore (Genesis 22) constitute a trio of innumerability promises across the patriarchal narrative, each using a different natural phenomenon beyond human enumeration.

---

### Council Note 10 — On 13:17: Walking the Land

**הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ ("walk about in the land / walk through the land"):** The Hithpael of הָלַךְ ("to walk") — reflexive and intensive — means "to walk about, to traverse repeatedly." This is the same verb form used of Enoch walking with God (5:22, 24: הִתְהַלֶּךְ-חֲנוֹךְ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים), and of God himself walking in the garden (3:8: מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן). The Hithpael conveys intimate, sustained, back-and-forth traversal — not a single passage through but a comprehensive, repeated moving through.

The act of walking the land's "length and breadth" (לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ) in the ancient Near East was a recognized symbolic gesture of territorial claim — the body moving through the land as a form of covenant title-deed. YHWH commands Abram to *walk* the land as the physical and embodied counterpart to the verbal promise. The word establishes possession; the feet confirm it.

The LXX Vaticanus renders **διόδευσον αὐτήν** — "traverse it" — a compound verb that captures the movement of passing through but loses the sustained, back-and-forth quality of the Hithpael. The MT is followed.

The council adopts "walk through the land, through its length and through its breadth" — LOGOS's version, which preserves the two-dimensional scope (length and breadth) without the slight awkwardness of EMET's "walk about in the land through its length and through its breadth."

---

### Council Note 11 — On 13:18: The Oaks of Mamre at Hebron

**אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("oaks of Mamre"):** The Hebrew אֵלֹן (elon, "oak" or possibly "terebinth/large tree") occurs at several sacred sites in the patriarchal narratives: the oak of Moreh at Shechem (12:6); the oaks of Mamre here; the oak at Shechem where Jacob buries the foreign gods (35:4). Sacred trees — particularly large, ancient oaks — mark sites of divine encounter in the religious geography of Canaan and Israel. Abram settles under a grove of oaks already associated with a sacred site.

**Singular vs. plural:** The MT reads the plural אֵלֹנֵי ("oaks of," a grove). The LXX Vaticanus renders **τὴν δρῦν τὴν Μαμβρη** — "the oak of Mambre" (singular). This is a minor textual variant; the council follows the MT plural: "the oaks of Mamre." The grove reading is more consistent with the site's later appearance in the narrative (Genesis 18:1, where God appears to Abraham "at the oaks of Mamre," again in the plural).

**Mamre and Hebron:** Mamre appears as both a place-name and a personal name (Genesis 14:13, 24 — Mamre the Amorite, ally of Abram). The site at Hebron (modern al-Khalīl, "the Friend" — a title for Abraham) will become the most significant patriarchal site in Canaan: here the three visitors will appear to Abraham (18:1–15); here Abraham will purchase the cave of Machpelah and bury Sarah (Genesis 23); here Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah will all be interred. The chapter's closing verse — Abram settling at Mamre, building an altar to the LORD — is the beginning of the patriarchal connection to the land that will become sacred through burial, promise, and divine presence.

**The third altar (12:7 → 12:8 → 13:18):** Abram has now built three altars in Canaan:
1. At the oak of Moreh, Shechem (12:7) — the first theophany
2. Between Bethel and Ai (12:8) — the first encampment in the hill country
3. At the oaks of Mamre, Hebron (13:18) — after the renewal of the divine promises

Each altar marks a moment of divine encounter or divine address. Abram stakes the land with worship rather than with military conquest. He possesses nothing he can legally defend — but he has built altars at Shechem, Bethel, and now Mamre. The land is being consecrated to YHWH before a single deed of ownership is recorded.

**The final contrast:** The chapter closes by juxtaposing, without comment, two tent-pitchings:
- Lot moved his tent "as far as Sodom" (v. 12) — toward the city the narrator has just characterized as "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly"
- Abram pitched his tent at the oaks of Mamre (v. 18) — near the place that will become the burial ground of the covenant family, and built an altar to the LORD

Two tents. Two trajectories. The text does not moralize. It simply shows.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Summary for Genesis 13

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | SOPHIA's Decision | Note |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------|------|
| 13:1 | וַיַּעַל ("went up") | Ἀνέβη ("went up") | "went up" — MT followed | Full agreement on directional verb |
| 13:2 | כָּבֵד מְאֹד ("very heavy") | πλούσιος σφόδρα ("very wealthy") | "very rich" — LXX sense followed | MT's כָּבֵד wordplay with 12:10 preserved in this note |
| 13:3 | לְמַסָּעָיו ("by his stages") | ἐπορεύθη ὅθεν ἦλθεν ("went from where he had come") | "journeyed on" — MT sense followed | LXX collapses the stage-itinerary formula |
| 13:7 | הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי | ὁ Χαναναῖος καὶ ὁ Φερεζαῖος | MT followed | Full agreement in substance |
| 13:10 | כְּגַן-יְהוָה ("like the garden of the LORD") | ὡς παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ ("like the paradise of God") | "the garden of the LORD" — MT followed | LXX uses "paradise" (παράδεισος); MT's "garden" is more intimate; Edenic resonance equivalent |
| 13:10 | שַׁחֵת יְהוָה ("the LORD destroyed") | καταστρέψαι τὸν θεόν ("God overthrew") | "the LORD destroyed" — MT followed | LXX uses θεός/θεόν for YHWH per standard LXX practice |
| 13:13 | לַיהוָה ("against the LORD") | ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before/against God") | "against the LORD" — MT followed | LXX's ἐναντίον slightly softens the adversarial preposition |
| 13:16 | כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ ("like the dust of the earth") | ὡς τὴν ἄμμον τῆς γῆς ("like the sand of the earth") | "dust of the earth" — MT followed | Most significant divergence in the chapter; dust carries Adam/creation resonance; LXX's sand loses this |
| 13:17 | הִתְהַלֵּךְ ("walk about / walk through") | διόδευσον αὐτήν ("traverse it") | "walk through" — MT followed | LXX loses the Hithpael's sustained traversal quality |
| 13:18 | אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("oaks of Mamre") | τὴν δρῦν τὴν Μαμβρη ("the oak of Mambre") | "oaks of Mamre" — MT plural followed | LXX singular "oak"; MT plural confirmed by 18:1 |

---

### Agreements Across All Three Translators

The following decisions were unanimous and required no council arbitration:

1. **"The LORD" for יְהוָה** — throughout the chapter, per established SOPHIA convention.
2. **"The plain of the Jordan" for כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן** — all three translators agree on this as the standard English equivalent for the Jordan valley floor.
3. **"Like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt"** — all three preserve the double simile exactly, following the MT over the LXX's "paradise of God."
4. **"Dust of the earth"** — all three follow the MT against the LXX's "sand of the earth" unanimously.
5. **The altar notices (vv. 4, 18)** — all three render substantially identically.
6. **The four directional terms (v. 14): "northward and southward and eastward and westward"** — unanimous.
7. **"Your offspring" for זַרְעֲךָ** — consistent with the ruling established at Genesis 12:7 (see GEN_012 disagreement report, Disagreement 8).
8. **"Separate yourself from me" for הִפָּרֶד נָא מֵעָלָי** — all three render substantially the same.
9. **The proleptic parenthesis at verse 10** — all three preserve "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah" as a parenthetical narrator's aside; no translator smooths or removes it.
10. **The MT throughout** — where LXX diverges, all three translators independently follow the MT at every significant point. The council confirms this unanimously.
